summary: Simon Späti explores building a complete open-source data warehouse architecture using Apache Druid for storage, Apache Superset for visualization, and Apache Airflow for orchestration. He explains why Druid's column-oriented, distributed design excels at OLAP workloads despite SQL join limitations. The post advocates for moving transformation logic into code-based orchestrators like Airflow rather than traditional ETL tools. He concludes that while these open-source tools work well together, enterprise users may still want to invest in commercial visualization tools since that's what end users actually see.
key_points: Druid provides sub-second OLAP queries and real-time streaming ingestion with a scalable cluster architecture of Middle-Manager, Historical, and Broker nodes
Apache Airflow represents a paradigm shift from traditional ETL tools by keeping transformation logic in code (Python DAGs) rather than encapsulated in proprietary tools
The combination of Druid, Airflow, and Superset creates a viable open-source alternative to commercial data warehouse stacks
Druid's main limitation is poor support for SQL joins and advanced SQL capabilities
Enterprise adoption of open-source orchestration naturally follows when companies start using open-source data technologies
money_quotes: Instead of encapsulating your critical transformation logic somewhere in a tool, you place it where it belongs to inside the Orchestrator.
In more modern architectures, these tools aren't enough anymore. Moreover, code and data transformation logic are much more valuable to other data-savvy people in the company.

My experience so far is that Druid is bloody fast and a perfect fit for OLAP cube replacements in a traditional way, but still needs a more relaxed startup to install clusters, ingest data, view logs etc.
Apache Airflow and its features as an orchestrator are something which has not happened much yet in traditional Business Intelligence environments.
I believe this change comes very naturally when you start using open-source and more new technologies.
As an enterprise company, you might want to spend some money in that category because that is what the users can see at the end of the day.
key_insight: Modern data architectures benefit from separating concerns across specialized open-source tools—columnar storage for speed, code-based orchestration for maintainability, and lightweight visualization for accessibility—rather than relying on monolithic proprietary ETL platforms.
